user_input:
Hi,
I have noticed that me and my colleague are unable to connect to the RCS. We both are connecting by SFTP, rcs.uis.cam.ac.uk on port 22 and both get the same unable to connect message.
We have also had a ticket from a user in our department explaining that she is also unable to connect where she was able to last week.
Is there a problem with the service at the moment? Or has anything changed that might be causing this?
Thanks
Kind Regards,
Shaun Cooke (he/him)
IT Service Manager
Department of Plant Sciences
University of Cambridge
Downing Street, Cambridge, CB2 3EA
Tel: 44 (0)1223 748962 | stc48@cam.ac.uk<[stc48@cam.ac.uk](mailto:stc48@cam.ac.uk)>
[https://www.plantsci.cam.ac.uk<https://www.plantsci.cam.ac.uk/](https://www.plantsci.cam.ac.uk%3Chttps://www.plantsci.cam.ac.uk/)>
This email (together with any files transmitted with it) is intended only for the use of the individual(s) to whom it is addressed. It may contain information which is confidential and/or legally privileged. If you have received this email in error, please notify the sender and delete the original message.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Access / RCS Connectivity / SSH-MFA
Technical Action Required: No (user onboarding guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports that multiple people are unable to connect to `rcs.uis.cam.ac.uk` over SFTP/SSH, raising the possibility of either a broader service problem or account-specific access changes. The correct first response is to confirm whether there is an active service incident and, if not, gather examples from affected users to determine whether the problem is shared or user-specific.

ACTION
1) Check whether there is an active service issue affecting RCS SSH/SFTP access.
2) Ask for example usernames, client/method used, and the exact error messages seen by affected users.
3) If the service is healthy, distinguish between users who cannot access RCS at all and users with account-specific denial errors.
4) For individual users, confirm SSH/storage MFA enrolment if relevant and ask them to raise their own tickets for account-specific checks.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Shaun,

We’re first checking whether there is any broader issue affecting RCS SSH/SFTP access.

If the service itself is healthy, we’ll need the affected usernames, the client or method being used, and the exact error messages so we can tell whether this is a shared problem or an account-specific one.

For users who can access the web portal but not SSH/SFTP services, we may also need to confirm that SSH/storage MFA has been set up separately.

If any individual user continues to have problems, please ask them to raise their own ticket so we can check their account details directly.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not troubleshoot third-party user accounts through one reporter without user-specific consent/context.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RCS Connection Issues
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The issue described is similar to previous incidents where users were unable to connect to RCS via SFTP [1][2][3]. These issues were resolved by the storage team, indicating a possible recurring problem.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Alert the storage team about the recurring connection issue.
2. Inform the user that the issue is being investigated and provide an estimated time for resolution if possible.
3. Monitor the status of the investigation and update the user accordingly.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Are there any specific error messages being displayed?
- Have there been any recent changes to the network or firewall settings?
- Are other services or applications experiencing similar connectivity issues?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Shaun,

Thank you for bringing this to our attention. We are aware of similar issues in the past and have alerted the storage team to investigate this matter. We will keep you updated on the progress and resolution.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the storage team is promptly informed of any connectivity issues to minimize downtime.
- Provide regular updates to the user to maintain transparency and trust.

[1] : HPCSSUP-29314
[2] : HPCSSUP-89893
[3] : HPCSSUP-91273

user_input:
Good morning, I am doing a benchmarking exercise on the ukaea-spr and ukaea-spr-hbm nodes.
I've managed to find most of the hardware info I need with some probing,
but for completeness I could do with some information as follows:
Network Interconnect
Network Topology
Storage Device Type (on the RDS filesystem) i.e. SSD/HDD/NVMe
Many Thanks in Advance,
Aleks
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: System Information / Hardware Specs
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User requested platform-level hardware details for benchmarking. Provided high-level interconnect/topology/storage architecture info.

ACTION
Provide requested specs:
- Interconnect: one Infiniband NDR 200 per node
- Topology: fat-tree
- Storage type: Lustre filesystem

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Aleks,

For `ukaea-spr-hbm`, the requested details are:
- Network interconnect: one Infiniband NDR 200 per node
- Network topology: fat tree
- Storage type (RDS): Lustre filesystem

Please let us know if you need anything further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Keep responses at approved/public detail level.

user_input:
Hi,
I have been able to log onto the VPN but when I try to login to Citrix Workspace I just get the error message attached. Are you able to help?
Best,
Dan
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP Access / Citrix Client Troubleshooting
Technical Action Required: No (diagnostic guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on VPN is unable to log in via the Citrix Workspace app. The appropriate first diagnostic is to verify whether browser-based launch through `anywhere.cam.ac.uk` works and whether the SRCP desktop can be started there, which helps separate a Citrix client problem from a broader access issue.

ACTION
1) Ask the user to test web launch via `https://anywhere.cam.ac.uk/`.
2) Verify whether the SRCP desktop is visible there and whether launching from the browser hands off to Citrix correctly.
3) If browser launch works, focus on the local Citrix Workspace client configuration or version.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Daniel,

While connected to VPN, please log in via:
https://anywhere.cam.ac.uk/

Do you see your SRCP virtual desktop there, and does launching from the browser open Citrix correctly?

Regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume Citrix backend fault without basic path validation.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Citrix Workspace / Login Issues
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues logging into Citrix Workspace despite being connected to the VPN. Similar issues have been resolved by resetting the Citrix program or ensuring the correct settings and application are used [1][2][4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Direct the user to reset the Citrix program by following the instructions at https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/srcp/swd/faq.html [1].
2. Ensure the user has the Citrix desktop app installed and the launch options on the browser are set to the app in the settings [2].
3. If the user is on an older OS, recommend upgrading to a supported version [3].
4. If the user is still experiencing issues, escalate to the SRCP team for further investigation [3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm if you’ve reset the Citrix program by following https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/srcp/swd/faq.html? [1]
- Please provide screenshots of any error messages you see. [1]
- What is your operating system and version? [3]
- Are you connected to the Cambridge VPN? [2]

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Dan,

Thank you for reaching out. To resolve the Citrix Workspace login issue, please follow these steps:

1. Reset the Citrix program by following the instructions at https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/srcp/swd/faq.html [1].
2. Ensure you have the Citrix desktop app installed and the launch options on the browser are set to the app in the settings [2].
3. If you are on an older OS, consider upgrading to a supported version [3].

If you continue to experience issues, please provide screenshots of any error messages and confirm your operating system and version.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user is connected to the Cambridge VPN before attempting to log in to Citrix Workspace [2].
- Recommend upgrading to a supported OS if the user is on an older version [3].

Dear Support Team,
I've recently added another DiRAC project (DP371) to my user account (rab200) via SAFE, but the resources are not showing up as available on CSD3/Wilkes. 'mybalance' doesn't show the project. I think the account code is probably "DIRAC-DP371-GPU" but I submissions with that account fail. Could help with this?
Cheers,
Richard
==========================================================
Dr. Richard Booth
Royal Society University Research Fellow & University Academic Fellow
School of Physics & Astronomy
W. H. Bragg Building
University of Leeds, UK
Email: r.a.booth@leeds.ac.uk<[r.a.booth@leeds.ac.uk](mailto:r.a.booth@leeds.ac.uk)>
My working hours may differ from yours
I do not expect a reply outside of your own working hours
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Account Provisioning / Project Membership
Technical Action Required: Yes (provisioning completion)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User added a DiRAC project in SAFE but it is not yet visible in `mybalance`. Provisioning is not immediate, so the next step is to check whether the account association is still pending and complete the internal provisioning workflow if required.

ACTION
1) Check provisioning status for requested project association.
2) Complete pending provisioning.
3) Once provisioning has completed, confirm that the account is visible and usable.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Richard,

Provisioning for this project is not automatic. We will check whether the project association is still pending on our side and complete the required provisioning step if necessary.

We will update you once that has been checked.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not treat SAFE change as instantly reflected in Slurm/accounting systems.

The user has added a new DiRAC project (DP371) to their account via SAFE but is unable to see the project resources on CSD3/Wilkes. This issue is similar to previous cases where users were not properly added to the project or the project was not correctly configured [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify if the user has been added to the DP371 project through SAFE.
2. Check if the user has been added to the correct account and partition combinations for DP371.
3. If the user is not properly added, add them to the necessary accounts and partitions.
4. Confirm with the user that they can now see the project resources and submit jobs successfully.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide a screenshot of your SAFE account showing the DP371 project?
- Have you tried logging out and back in to refresh your connection?
- What is the exact error message you receive when trying to submit jobs with the "DIRAC-DP371-GPU" account?

user_input:
Dear HPC Team,
I am currently using the DAWN cluster and would like to leverage the Intel GPUs for running my PyTorch code.
According to the documentation, the following setup works well:
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate pytorch-gpu
And in Python:
import torch
import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex
...
1. Enable GPU
model = model.to('xpu')
data = data.to('xpu')
model = ipex.optimize(model, dtype=torch.float32)
This setup functions as expected.
However, the pytorch-gpu environment appears to be a system-provided environment. For the purposes of my project, I would like to create and manage my own local Conda environment that is compatible with the Intel GPUs (i.e., includes PyTorch and the Intel Extension for PyTorch with xpu support).
I have attempted to clone the pytorch-gpu environment as well as install all of its modules manually, but I run into repeated HTTP 403 errors when trying to fetch packages from the Intel Conda channel. This seems to indicate restricted access or deprecation of some packages or channels.
Could you please advise on the recommended way to:
1.  Create a local Conda environment compatible with the Intel GPU setup used on DAWN,
2.  Ensure installation of all necessary packages without running into access issues?
If there is a supported method to export or replicate the pytorch-gpu environment into a user-managed Conda environment, that would be ideal.
Thank you very much for your assistance!
Best wishes,
Moritz

Category: Software Environment / Dawn PVC / PyTorch-IPEX
Technical Action Required: No (user setup guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User wants a modifiable local environment equivalent to centrally managed `pytorch-gpu`. Central env is immutable. Supported options:
- activate central `pytorch-gpu` then layer user virtualenv, or
- build user conda env with compatible pinned versions from Intel channel and test on PVC nodes.

ACTION
1) Confirm central env baseline works.
2) Explain central env cannot be modified.
3) Provide two supported approaches:
   - `pytorch-gpu` + user virtualenv layering
   - standalone conda env with compatible pins (`intel-extension-for-pytorch`, `pytorch`, python range) and Intel repo
4) Require testing on PVC/PVC9 interactive node.
5) Set required env vars for XPU runtime test.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Moritz,

`pytorch-gpu` is centrally managed and cannot be modified directly.

Supported options are:
1) Activate `pytorch-gpu`, then activate your own virtualenv for extra packages.
2) Create your own conda env with compatible versions (e.g. pinned `intel-extension-for-pytorch` + `pytorch`) and test it on PVC nodes.

Please use the Dawn guidance here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc.html#machine-learning-data-science-frameworks

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not advise modifying centrally managed environments.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Environment Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
Creating a local Conda environment compatible with Intel GPUs on DAWN involves specific steps and considerations. The user has encountered issues with HTTP 403 errors when trying to fetch packages from the Intel Conda channel, indicating restricted access or deprecation of some packages or channels.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's access to the necessary Conda channels and packages.
2. Provide guidance on creating a local Conda environment compatible with Intel GPUs.
3. Ensure the user is aware of the correct module loading sequence and environment activation steps.

ACTION
Direct the user to the following steps for creating a local Conda environment compatible with Intel GPUs on DAWN:

1. Load the necessary modules:
   ```
   module purge
   module load rhel9/default-dawn
   module load intelpython-conda/2025.0
   ```

2. Activate the pre-made PyTorch environment:
   ```
   conda activate pytorch-gpu-2.3.1
   ```

3. Clone the environment to a local path:
   ```
   conda create --yes --prefix "${FULL_VENV_NAME}" --clone pytorch-gpu-2.3.1
   ```
   Replace `${FULL_VENV_NAME}` with the full path to the folder for the new environment.

4. Verify the environment by running a sanity check on a compute node:
   ```
   python -c "import torch; import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex; print(torch.version); print(ipex.version); [print(f'[{i}]: {torch.xpu.get_device_properties(i)}') for i in range(torch.xpu.device_count())];"
   ```

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Moritz,

To create a local Conda environment compatible with Intel GPUs on DAWN, please follow these steps:

1. Load the necessary modules:
   ```
   module purge
   module load rhel9/default-dawn
   module load intelpython-conda/2025.0
   ```

2. Activate the pre-made PyTorch environment:
   ```
   conda activate pytorch-gpu-2.3.1
   ```

3. Clone the environment to a local path:
   ```
   conda create --yes --prefix "${FULL_VENV_NAME}" --clone pytorch-gpu-2.3.1
   ```
   Replace `${FULL_VENV_NAME}` with the full path to the folder for the new environment.

4. Verify the environment by running a sanity check on a compute node:
   ```
   python -c "import torch; import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex; print(torch.version); print(ipex.version); [print(f'[{i}]: {torch.xpu.get_device_properties(i)}') for i in range(torch.xpu.device_count())];"
   ```

If you encounter any issues or have further questions, please let us know.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user has the necessary permissions and access to the required Conda channels and packages.
- Do not guess or provide untested solutions without verified evidence.

`
module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn      
module load intel-oneapi-mkl/2025.1.0/oneapi/hc67rita
module load intelpython-conda/2025.0  
module list
conda activate pytorch-gpu-2.3.1 
conda info --envs
conda create --yes --prefix "${FULL_VENV_NAME}" --clone pytorch-gpu-2.3.
`
where `FULL_VENV_NAME` is the Full path to the folder for the new env to go into. 
Did you run the sanity-check on a compute node?

Hi
I am at a bit of a loss as to how access storage provided by AIRR/Dawn?
My storage name is airr-p12-storage
My user id is dn-stan1.
I have looked extensively in the support/help docs but must be looking straight past this information.
Thanks
Phill
–
Prof Phillip J. Stansfeld (he/him)
Co-Director of the Sir Howard Dalton Centre | Professor in Computational Biochemistry | School of Life Sciences & Department of Chemistry | University of Warwick

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage Path Discovery
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User could not locate AIRR/Dawn storage path in docs. The exact project path is user/project-specific and should be discovered from the user account rather than assumed.

ACTION
1) Ask user to check available RDS links under home:
   - `ls -ld ~/rds/*`
2) Ask user to identify the AIRR project path from quota/project listing:
   - `quota`
3) Confirm the matching path format once identified (typically `~/rds/rds-<project-id>` symlink).
4) Clarify that `~/rds/...` is a convenience link to the underlying project path.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Phil,

Your AIRR storage path is user/project specific. Please run:

`ls -ld ~/rds/*`
`quota`

This will show the project link available in your account (typically `~/rds/rds-<project-id>`).  
That entry is a symlink to the underlying project storage location.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not hardcode project-specific paths unless verified for that user/account.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeking access to AIRR/Dawn storage. The retrieved documents suggest that access to project storage is typically found in the user's ~/rds/ directory after logging in [1][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's access to the AIRR/Dawn storage by checking their ~/rds/ directory.
2. If the user does not have access, ensure they are part of the AIRR allocation and have accepted the T&Cs [5].
3. If necessary, escalate to the RCS Team for further assistance [1][3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you tried logging out and logging back in to refresh your session? [1][3]
- Can you confirm that you have accepted the storage T&Cs at https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/terms.html? [5]
- Please provide the output of `ls -l ~/rds` to verify the directory contents.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Phill,

To access your AIRR/Dawn storage, please follow these steps:

1. Log out and log back in to refresh your session.
2. Check your ~/rds/ directory for the storage link.

If you do not see the storage link, please ensure you have accepted the storage T&Cs at https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/terms.html.

If you still encounter issues, please provide the output of `ls -l ~/rds` so we can further assist you.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user has accepted the storage T&Cs before granting access [5].
- Do not manually grant access without verifying the user's allocation and T&Cs acceptance.

user_input:
Hi -
I’ve just sbatched a job on the HPC, using cclake, job 9082481_[1-2]. If I squeue -u has27 it tells me (under NODELIST) 'ReqNodeNotAvail, Reserved for maintenance’. But maintenance finished last week.
Can you tell me what (if anything) I’ve done wrong? Script below.
Thanks,
Hamish
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -A GLOVER-SL3-CPU
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --mail-type=NONE
#SBATCH -p cclake  # name of the partition to run job on
#SBATCH -o /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/bowtie2/bowtie2%a.log
#SBATCH -c 8         # number of CPUs. Default: 1
#SBATCH --mem=27360M     # RAM memory.
#SBATCH -t 12:00:00  # time for the job HH:MM:SS. Default: 1 min
#SBATCH -a 1-2
source ~/.bashrc
1. activate conda environment
conda activate bowtie2
cd /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/soapnuke/clean
1. List all files in the folder starting with A or B,
2. then get the relevant line
INDIV_ID=$(ls -d [AB]* | head -n ${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID} | tail -n 1)
cd $INDIV_ID
READ1=$(ls *1.fq.gz)
READ2=$(ls *2.fq.gz)
cd /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/Genomes/LSRagtag
bowtie2 -p 8 -x LSRagtag -1 /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/soapnuke/clean/$INDIV_ID/$READ1 -2 /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/soapnuke/clean/$INDIV_ID/$READ2 | samtools sort > /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/align-LSRagtag/$INDIV_ID.sorted.bam
—
Dr Hamish Symington
Junior Research Fellow, Queens’ College, Cambridge
Academic Skills Tutor and Bye-Fellow, Downing College, Cambridge
Postdoctoral Researcher in the Glover Lab, Department of Plant Sciences, University of Cambridge
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm Scheduling Status Interpretation
Technical Action Required: No (explanation + minor script advice)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User saw scheduler reason text and assumed there was a post-maintenance fault. From the script provided, there is no obvious submission syntax problem, and this kind of reason string can still appear while a job is simply pending under normal scheduler priority. Additional optimisation: explicit `--mem` is redundant for cclake in this case.

ACTION
1) Check live job state and reason (`squeue`, `scontrol`).
2) Explain pending-by-priority behavior.
3) Suggest removing redundant `--mem` directive when CPU request already implies desired memory.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Hamish,

From the script you sent, the job appears to have submitted correctly and may simply be pending under normal scheduler priority.

For this script, `--mem=27360M` is redundant if you already request the corresponding CPU count on `cclake`; removing it can simplify scheduling requests.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Distinguish temporary reason strings from real submission errors.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: HPC / Job Scheduling
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with job scheduling on the cclake partition, with the error message 'ReqNodeNotAvail, Reserved for maintenance'. This is likely due to ongoing maintenance or node issues, as seen in similar cases [1][2][3][4][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify if there is any ongoing maintenance on the cclake partition.
2. Check the status of the nodes in the cclake partition to identify any issues.
3. If maintenance is ongoing, inform the user and provide an estimated time for completion.
4. If no maintenance is ongoing, investigate the specific nodes causing the issue and take appropriate action.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm if you have received any notifications about ongoing maintenance?
- Have you tried submitting the job to a different partition, such as icelake?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Hamish,

Thank you for your message. The issue you're experiencing is likely due to ongoing maintenance or node issues on the cclake partition. We are currently investigating this and will provide an update as soon as possible.

In the meantime, you can try submitting your job to a different partition, such as icelake, to see if that resolves the issue.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always verify the status of the partition and nodes before providing a resolution.
- Inform the user of any ongoing maintenance and provide an estimated time for completion.

user_input:
Dear sir/madam,
I have been told to contact this email to ask for an MFA because I see the error: ‘When performing ssh to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk the QR code is replaced either by garbage or is broken up by blank lines, making it unusable.’.
Thank you very much for sorting.
Many thanks,
James
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Enrollment / Client Rendering Issue
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
Mac terminal rendering can distort SSH QR code during MFA enrollment. Enrollment can still proceed by scanning if possible, or manually entering the secret key in authenticator app.

ACTION
1) Confirm issue is known for some Mac terminal setups.
2) Provide manual secret-key fallback method.
3) Clarify expected token labels for SSH vs web.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi James,

This is a known issue on some Mac setups where the QR may render poorly. You can still complete setup by manually entering the secret key in your authenticator app if scanning fails.

Please follow:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html

The SSH token should appear as `CSD3: SSH Login` (distinct from login-web token naming).

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not reset MFA unnecessarily when manual enrollment path is available.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with the MFA QR code during SSH login to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk. This is a known issue and may require an MFA reset [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify if the user has a valid CSD3 account [1].
2. Check if the user has already tried entering the secret key manually [2].
3. If the issue persists, schedule a quick MS Teams call to verify the user's photo ID for an MFA reset [2].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you tried entering the secret key manually as described in the documentation? [2]
- Are you using the correct SSH command format (e.g., `ssh username@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`)? [1][3]

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello James,

Thank you for reaching out. The issue you're experiencing with the MFA QR code is known and may require an MFA reset.

Please try the following steps:
1. Ensure you are using the correct SSH command format: `ssh username@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk` [1][3].
2. If the QR code is not working, you can enter the secret key manually as described in the documentation [2].

If the issue persists, we will need to schedule a quick MS Teams call to verify your photo ID for an MFA reset. Please let us know a convenient time for this call.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user has a valid CSD3 account before proceeding with an MFA reset [1].
- Verify the user's photo ID during the MS Teams call for security purposes [2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-54226
[2] : HPCSSUP-94321
[3] : HPCSSUP-50399

user_input:
Hi Support Team,
I’m working on some SRCNet related projects and am attempting to run a containerised workflow on Azimuth which requires both CUDA and MPI support. I have built the required software built into an Ubuntu container with specific CUDA and MPI versions, but these of course need to be compatible with what is possible to deploy on the instance itself.
Is there a guide to how to set these up correctly? Or some experience with running GPU and MPI codes on Azimuth that I can leverage so that we can progress the SRC work?
I’m happy to provide the Dockerfile so that you can see how things are being built, and information about the code I’m trying to run, if useful.
Kind regards,
Bradley Meyers
Support Scientist (Time Domain) | Australian SKA Regional Centre (AusSRC)
Research Fellow | Curtin Institute of Radio Astronomy (CIRA)
International Centre for Radio Astronomy Research (ICRAR)
Curtin University

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Cloud VM Configuration / GPU Containers / MPI
Technical Action Required: No (guidance)
Escalation Required: Optional (if capacity constrained)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User needed guidance for CUDA+MPI in Azimuth GPU VM. Recommended approach: install CUDA toolkit and Nvidia driver in VM, prefer Singularity for HPC container runtime with GPU bindings, and choose MPI runtime inside container as needed. Capacity caveat: GPU pool may be fully utilised.

ACTION
1) Confirm current GPU capacity availability.
2) Advise VM-level CUDA/driver install for GPU instances.
3) Recommend Singularity/Apptainer over Docker for HPC runtime.
4) Explain MPI runtime can be selected within container image.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Bradley,

On Azimuth GPU VMs, you can install the CUDA toolkit and NVIDIA open-source driver in the usual way. For HPC workflows we generally recommend Singularity/Apptainer rather than Docker, including GPU bindings to expose host GPUs.

MPI runtime selection is up to your container build choice.

Please note GPU capacity may currently be fully allocated, so provisioning can be constrained.

Thanks,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply immediate GPU availability when capacity is saturated.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: GPU / MPI Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is working on SRCNet related projects and needs to run a containerised workflow on Azimuth with CUDA and MPI support. They have built a Ubuntu container with specific CUDA and MPI versions but need guidance on setting these up correctly on Azimuth.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Review the user's Dockerfile and any relevant scripts to understand the specific CUDA and MPI versions and configurations.
2. Provide guidance on loading the appropriate modules on Azimuth to match the container's environment.
3. Suggest using the `openmpi-4.0.6-gcc-9.3.0-pwlkvaa` module, which is CUDA-aware and optimised for the Mellanox fabric [1][2].
4. Ensure the user is aware of the correct `srun` commands for launching MPI jobs on Azimuth.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Bradley,

To ensure compatibility between your container and the Azimuth environment, please follow these steps:

1. Load the appropriate modules on Azimuth:
```java
module use /usr/local/software/spack/current/share/spack/tcl/linux-scientific7-x86_64
module load openmpi-4.0.6-gcc-9.3.0-pwlkvaa
```
2. Use the following `srun` command to launch your MPI job:
```java
srun -p pascal --gres=gpu:2 -A RAU-SL3-GPU --ntasks=6 --nodes 2 --ntasks-per-node=3 python3 mpitest.py
```
3. Ensure your Dockerfile and scripts are configured to use the loaded modules and environment variables.

Please let us know if you encounter any issues or need further assistance.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user is aware of the correct module loading and `srun` commands for Azimuth.
- Do not guess module names or commands without verified evidence.

2}, 'source_weights': {'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': "[TICKET_SUMMARY] Optimised GPU-aware OpenMPI on CSD3 

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi,
I am currently working on CSD3 on a code that requires MPI and GPU together. Initially, I used a spack implementation that built its own version of CUDA and OpenMPI for that. However, due to these libraries and the environment not being optimised to run on CSD3,  the GPU performance I got was worse than on a single CPU core.
I tried to fix this by setting spack to use the locally installed CUDA toolkit (3hnxhjt) and OpenMPI (epagguv), which are the default ones that come on the ampere default module script. This did improve the single-rank run results, where now I can get higher performance than CPUs. However, the GPU performance is much worse when running more than one MPI rank, regardless of whether these ranks are across more than one GPU or within a single GPU. What is seemingly happening is that every time two GPU ranks need to exchange data in any way, they do so by sending it to the Host and then back to the relevant Device, as opposed to directly communicating (again, even if both ranks are in the same GPU).
It seems to me that this might be happening because the default OpenMPI that comes in the ampere module script has not been built with CUDA (according to its spack build instructions), and I could not find any OpenMPI that was built with CUDA and optimised for CSD3. I'm sure that there must be one somewhere, but maybe I'm just not finding it. I would like to ask what is the right OpenMPI to use in this case and if there are any suggested environment variables or MPI wrapper scripts that I should use. I'd also e happy to share my performance plots and profiles if necessary. Thank you for the support, and have a great day!
Best wishes,
Henrique

[dc-boyl1@login-e-5 benchmarks]$ more slurm-16538636.out

###################################
1. Grid directory and support directory
####################################
export prefix=$HOME/prefix
export grid=$HOME/Grid-HMC
mkdir $prefix
mkdir $grid
module load gcc/6
module load cuda/10.0
##################
#LIME
##################
cd $prefix
wget [http://usqcd-software.github.io/downloads/c-lime/lime-1.3.2.tar.gz](http://usqcd-software.github.io/downloads/c-lime/lime-1.3.2.tar.gz)
tar xvzf lime-1.3.2.tar.gz
cd lime-1.3.2
./configure --prefix $prefix
make all install
##################
#GMP - tesseract is badly configured
##################
cd $prefix
wget [https://ftp.gnu.org/gnu/gmp/gmp-6.1.2.tar.bz2](https://ftp.gnu.org/gnu/gmp/gmp-6.1.2.tar.bz2)
tar xvjf gmp-6.1.2.tar.bz2
cd gmp-6.1.2
./configure --prefix $prefix
make all install
##################
#MPFR - summit is badly configured
##################
cd $prefix
wget [https://www.mpfr.org/mpfr-current/mpfr-4.0.2.tar.gz](https://www.mpfr.org/mpfr-current/mpfr-4.0.2.tar.gz)
tar xvzf mpfr-4.0.2.tar.gz
cd mpfr-4.0.2
./configure --prefix $prefix --with-gmp=$prefix
make all install
###############
1. FFTW
###############
cd $prefix
wget [http://www.fftw.org/fftw-3.3.8.tar.gz](http://www.fftw.org/fftw-3.3.8.tar.gz)
tar xvzf fftw-3.3.8.tar.gz
cd fftw-3.3.8
./configure --prefix $prefix  --enable-avx2 --enable-float
##################
#OpenSSL
##################
cd $prefix
wget  [https://www.openssl.org/source/openssl-1.1.0l.tar.gz](https://www.openssl.org/source/openssl-1.1.0l.tar.gz)
tar xvzf openssl-1.1.0l.tar.gz
cd openssl-1.1.0l
./config --prefix=$prefix 
make all install
##################
#Obtain Grid and support codes
##################
cd $grid
git clone [https://paboyle@github.com/paboyle/Grid](https://paboyle@github.com/paboyle/Grid)
cd Grid
./bootstrap.sh
#########################
1. build for GPU
#########################
mkdir build-gpu
cd build-gpu
module load gcc/6
module load cuda/10.1
module load mvapich2-GDR/2.3-cuda10.1
export MPICXX=mpicxx 
export CXX=nvcc 
export CXXFLAGS="-arch=sm_60 -gencode=arch=compute_60,code=compute_60
-Xcompiler -fopenmp -I$prefix/include" 
export LIBS="-lmpi"
export LDFLAGS="-L$prefix/lib" 
export LD_LIBRARY_PATH=$prefix/lib/:$LD_LIBRARY_PATH
../configure \
--enable-precision=single \
--enable-simd=GPU \
--enable-comms=mpi3-auto  \
--enable-gen-simd-width=32 \
--enable-openmp 
make -j 24

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello,
This email is a follow up from our meeting with the Dawn team on 6th October with Eduardo Veiga, Deepak Aggarwal, Simon Hartley, and Karl Harrison.
MPI VERSION
We mentioned something about the MPI version between inside and outside the container. Inside the container the version is
Intel(R) MPI Library for Linux* OS, Version 2021.14 Build 20241121 (id: e7829d6)
Copyright 2003-2024, Intel Corporation.
Outside the container, on a Dawn node (after the appropriate module loads) the version is
Intel(R) MPI Library for Linux* OS, Version 2021.15 Build 20250213 (id: d233448)
Copyright 2003-2025, Intel Corporation.
[Note that on the login node the version matches the one in the apptainer container
Intel(R) MPI Library for Linux* OS, Version 2021.14 Build 20240911 (id: b3fc682)
Copyright 2003-2024, Intel Corporation.
Also the version before appropriate module loads (on the login node) is much older
Intel(R) MPI Library for Linux* OS, Version 2021.6 Build 20220227 (id: 28877f3f32)
Copyright 2003-2022, Intel Corporation.
However I never run mpi from the base environment so this should not be relevant.]
APPTAINER SETUP
In terms of our apptainer configuration, this is our definition file
Bootstrap: docker
From: intel/intel-extension-for-pytorch:2.7.10-xpu
%arguments
WORK_DIR=/home/sara
GCC_DIR=/home/sara/results/tmp
%files
1. Copy script files into the container
    entrypoint.sh {{ WORK_DIR }}/entrypoint.sh
    paths.sh {{ WORK_DIR }}/paths.sh
2. Specify the required Python libraries
    requirements.txt {{ WORK_DIR }}/requirements.txt
%environment
export TMPDIR="{{ GCC_DIR }}"
%post
apt-get update && \
apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
nfs-common \
pdsh \
openssh-server \
openssh-client \
software-properties-common \
build-essential \
autotools-dev \
net-tools \
python3-pip
cd {{ WORK_DIR }}
1. Install the required Python libraries
    pip install -r {{ WORK_DIR }}/requirements.txt
2. Set permissions
    chmod a+x {{ WORK_DIR }}/entrypoint.sh
%runscript
1. Hand off to the entrypoint script
    exec {{ WORK_DIR }}/entrypoint.sh
And this is my apptainer run command (I do not think fakeroot is strictly necessary, it is leftover from when I was trying to get ssh to work).
apptainer run --no-eval \
--no-init \
--no-umask \
--containall \
--rocm \
--writable-tmpfs \
--fakeroot \
--mount type=bind,src=code,dst=/home/sara/code \
--mount type=bind,src=/home/dn-vene1/rds/rds-airr-p11-3Xuj7acUv1g/results,dst=/home/sara/results \
--mount type=bind,src=/home/dn-vene1/rds/rds-airr-p11-3Xuj7acUv1g/bert_data,dst=/home/sara/data \
--env-file=env \
image.sif
The above works for single node, where we are using mpiexec from inside the container. When trying to get multi-node working (for which I launch multiple containers using mpi outside the container), I end up having to pass various bits and bobs from the environment outside (e.g. LD_LIBRARY_PATH, I_MPI_ROOT, PMI_RANK, PMI_SIZE etc etc – not sure if any of them are strictly necessary as it is still not working, but definitely it is very complain-y about not finding libfabric if the paths are not pointing in the correct direction).

user_input:
Dear Sir/Madam,
I hope you are doing well.
I am writing to inquire about activating a Conda environment in Jupyter
Notebook within the Login-Web interface. I have already created a Conda
environment with ipykernel installed. However, when I start a Jupyter
Notebook session, I am unable to switch the kernel to my Conda environment.
Could you please provide guidance on how to properly activate and use my
Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook?
Thank you for your time and assistance.
Best regards,
Patrick
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Jupyter / Login-Web / User Environment
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User could not select a conda environment kernel in Login-Web Jupyter. Resolution is to ensure the environment has `ipykernel` and is explicitly registered as a Jupyter kernel, then restart/reopen the notebook session.

ACTION
1) Confirm environment prerequisites
   - Environment exists and can be activated.
   - `ipykernel` is installed in that environment.
2) Register the environment as a Jupyter kernel
   - Activate env and run:
     `python -m ipykernel install --user --name <env_name> --display-name "Python (<env_name>)"`
3) Ask user to restart/reopen Login-Web Jupyter session and select the new kernel.
4) If kernel still does not appear, collect diagnostics:
   - `jupyter kernelspec list`
   - exact env activation/install commands used.
5) Share official docs for full workflow/reference.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Patrick,

Please register your conda environment as a Jupyter kernel, then restart/reopen your Login-Web Jupyter session.

Example:

```bash
conda activate <env_name>
python -m ipykernel install --user --name <env_name> --display-name "Python (<env_name>)"
```

After that, select `Python (<env_name>)` from the Jupyter kernel menu.

Full guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html#adding-virtual-environments

If it still doesn’t appear, please share:
- `jupyter kernelspec list`
- the exact commands you used to create and register the environment.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Keep instructions aligned with the documented Login-Web kernel registration workflow.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Jupyter Notebook
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is having trouble activating a Conda environment within a Jupyter Notebook session on the Login-Web interface. The issue is likely related to the kernel configuration for the Conda environment [1][3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm that the user has installed the `ipykernel` package in their Conda environment [1].
2. Verify that the user has registered the Conda environment as a Jupyter kernel using the command `ipython kernel install --user --name=[conda_venv_name]` [1].
3. Guide the user to follow the steps outlined in the documentation for setting up Jupyter on CSD3, specifically the section on adding virtual environments [3].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Patrick,

To activate and use your Conda environment in a Jupyter Notebook on the Login-Web interface, please follow these steps:

1. Ensure that you have installed the `ipykernel` package in your Conda environment. You can do this by running:
   ```
   pip install ipykernel
   ```
2. Register the Conda environment as a Jupyter kernel by running:
   ```
   ipython kernel install --user --name=[conda_venv_name]
   ```
3. Follow the steps outlined in the documentation for setting up Jupyter on CSD3, specifically the section on adding virtual environments: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html#adding-virtual-environments](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html#adding-virtual-environments) [3].

Please let us know if you encounter any issues during these steps.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user follows the documented steps for setting up Jupyter on CSD3 to avoid configuration issues [3].
- Do not manually modify kernel configurations without following the documented procedure [1].

2. ```

```
2. Once done, run 
```
ipython kernel install --user --name=<virtual environment name>
```
3. Spin up a login-web jupyter session and follow from step 8 on this guide https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html#adding-virtual-environments

user_input:
Dear whom it may concern,
project id: pnk0p5ltryy
I hope this email finds you well. I am using RStudio as directed through RDS Farm. However, when I try to install packages(basic ones like dplyr and tidyverse),error occurs saying "error reading from connection". I tried to use web to access CRAN, but it seems I don't have internet connection on the virtual workspace. I would really appreciate your guidance, thank you very much.
Sincerely,
Yira
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP / Package Management / Restricted Network
Technical Action Required: Yes (staff-managed package availability)
Escalation Required: Yes (SRCP team)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on SRCP RStudio could not install CRAN packages due to no external internet by design. SRCP policy requires curated package availability managed by staff; users may need to use internal repo mirror and request missing packages.

ACTION
1) Explain SRCP security model (no direct external repo access).
2) Escalate to SRCP team for package provisioning.
3) Ask user for package list.
4) Provide internal repo usage (`options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")`).
5) Track/fork to dedicated package request ticket if needed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Yira,

On SRCP, direct access to external package repositories is restricted by design. Please use the internal R repo:

options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")
available.packages()

If required packages are still missing, please send the exact package list and we can add them through the SRCP process.

Regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not suggest bypassing SRCP network restrictions.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RDS Farm / RStudio
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with installing R packages in RStudio via the RDS Farm, likely due to internet access restrictions. Similar issues have been resolved by configuring the internal repository [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify if the user is using the internal repository for R packages.
2. If not, instruct the user to configure the repository using:
   ```R
   options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")
   available.packages()
   ```
3. If the issue persists, escalate to the SRCP team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm if you have configured the internal repository using the following commands?
  ```R
  options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")
  available.packages()
  ```
- Are you still encountering the same error after configuring the repository?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Yira,

Thank you for your message. The issue you're experiencing with installing R packages in RStudio via the RDS Farm is likely due to internet access restrictions. Please configure the internal repository using the following commands:
```R
options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")
available.packages()
```
If you continue to encounter issues, please let us know.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user is aware of the internal repository configuration to avoid internet access issues [1].
- Do not manually install packages without verifying the repository configuration.

Elizabeth

Dear HPC support,
I am a PhD student with Pietro Lio project cs181 which has access to the Ampere node partition.
I have a need for some CPU only runs on the icelake nodes and was wondering if there's anyway to get this?
Many thanks
James Rowbottom - jr908
________________________________
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 09 December 2024 17:25
To: James Rowbottom <jr908@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: New Tier2 Account on CSD3 (jr908)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Before you login for the first time, please use your SSH
public key to login via SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
Once MFA is configured, please use your SSH public key to
login via SSH to
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge
Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your
project's scratch directory (rds-t2-cs181). Please note that
this is shared per project so each user will probably wish to create
their own subdirectory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjr908%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cb2a72e3346134cb8757b08dd187683f7%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638693619533696586%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=GUPkCeyukD4EZY8jc2oXOIpFpIpHahjHvOyx2dOSqx4%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjr908%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cb2a72e3346134cb8757b08dd187683f7%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638693619533696586%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=GUPkCeyukD4EZY8jc2oXOIpFpIpHahjHvOyx2dOSqx4%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The project listed by mybalance is the project you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A t2-cs181-XXX
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A t2-cs181-XXX ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for DiRAC jobs are currently:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
Please note that currently:
Cascade Lake uses a RHEL7-compatible O/S
Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
This combination of RHEL7 and RHEL8 will become pure RHEL8 in time.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7
Cascade Lake login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Cascade Lake cluster. login-icelake will take you
to a different set of RHEL8 login nodes best suited for development
work for Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24

As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the

Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.

Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (sh2001)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
Please note that currently:
Cascade Lake uses a RHEL7-compatible O/S
Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
This combination of RHEL7 and RHEL8 will become pure RHEL8 in time.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7
Cascade Lake login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Cascade Lake cluster. login-icelake will take you
to a different set of RHEL8 login nodes best suited for development
work for Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a mixture of RHEL7 and RHEL8-compatible
operating systems. We recommend that all application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, please use
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk (aka login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk).
For development on icelake*, sapphire* and ampere* partitions, please use
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. The module names contain hashes uniquely
identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be
searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for
modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing
builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Csh2001%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C45f1802bed5f4794f55108dc54b19708%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638478368922986884%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=CWOdOMH0UPXG%2FbLcyKonQ8u7LWG%2Bi%2BDUA6zg0BQ6u4M%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Csh2001%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C45f1802bed5f4794f55108dc54b19708%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638478368922986884%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=CWOdOMH0UPXG%2FbLcyKonQ8u7LWG%2Bi%2BDUA6zg0BQ6u4M%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3420MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (pmwd2)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3) facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8, where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on that type) you may alternatively SSH to login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes, but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system but present additional development packages tuned to the node hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module (hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack see [http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html).)
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

Hi ,
I am writing to seek your assistance with logging into the Dirac machine. I encountered a similar issue before.
I have completed all the initial setup steps and obtained the QR code from the web portal token some time ago. However, it has been a while since I last logged into the machine. While I can still access the safe account, I am unable to log in to the web portal( ( Link<[https://id.hpc.cam.ac.uk/auth/realms/CSD3/protocol/openid-connect/auth?response_type=code&scope=openid&client_id=login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk&state=D0OAg-WHtN1N7fTXG-9dpjl1oOY&redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Flogin-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk%2Foidc&nonce=equ0MBTPKf0h82CBRborxJ-OMzVTwl-UTQPZ2TuGe4s](https://id.hpc.cam.ac.uk/auth/realms/CSD3/protocol/openid-connect/auth?response_type=code&scope=openid&client_id=login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk&state=D0OAg-WHtN1N7fTXG-9dpjl1oOY&redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Flogin-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk%2Foidc&nonce=equ0MBTPKf0h82CBRborxJ-OMzVTwl-UTQPZ2TuGe4s)> ).
Could you please help me resolve this issue?
Thank you for your support.
Best regards,
Karthika
